elements.sent-2411-001: In multi-agent systems, the agent behavior is highly influenced by its utility function, as these utilities shape both individual goals as well as interactions with the other agents.
elements.sent-2411-002: Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) is a well-established approach to inferring the utility function by observing an expert behavior within a given environment.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we extend the IRL framework to the multi-agent setting, assuming to observe agents who are following Nash Equilibrium (NE) policies.
elements.sent-2411-004: We theoretically investigate the set of utilities that explain the behavior of NE experts.
elements.sent-2411-005: Specifically, we provide an explicit characterization of the feasible reward set and analyze how errors in estimating the transition dynamics and expert behavior impact the recovered rewards.
elements.sent-2411-006: Building on these findings, we provide the first sample complexity analysis for the multi-agent IRL problem.
elements.sent-2411-007: Finally, we provide a numerical evaluation of our theoretical results.
elements.sent-2411-008: Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning has gathered significant interest in recent years due to its ability to model scenarios involving interacting agents.
elements.sent-2411-009: Notable successes have been achieved in domains such as autonomous driving~\citep{DBLP:journals/corr/Shalev-ShwartzS16a, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2010-09776 , internet marketing~\citep{Jin_2018 , multi-robot control~\citep{dawood2023safemultiagentreinforcementlearning , traffic control~\citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1908-03761 , and multi-player games~\citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1909-07528,samvelyan2019starcraftmultiagentchallenge .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: All these applications require carefully designed reward functions, which is challenging even in single-agent settings~\citep{DBLP:journals/corr/AmodeiOSCSM16, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1711-02827 and becomes more complex in multi-agent environments where each agent's reward function must be tailored to their specific, potentially different, goals.}
elements.sent-2411-011: In many scenarios, it is possible to observe an expert demonstrating optimal behavior, yet the underlying reward function guiding this behavior remains unknown.
elements.sent-2411-012: This is where IRL~\citep{10.5555/645529.657801 becomes crucial.}
elements.sent-2411-013: IRL aims to recover feasible reward functions that can rationalize the observed behavior as optimal.
elements.sent-2411-014: However, the initial work in IRL revealed a fundamental challenge: the problem is ill-posed because multiple reward functions can potentially explain the same behavior.
elements.sent-2411-015: To address this, subsequent research has focused on reformulating the IRL problem to make it more practical and applicable in real-world settings~\citep{10.1145/1015330.1015430,10.5555/1620270.1620297,10.5555/1625275.1625692,Ratliff2006BoostingSP,NIPS2011_c51ce410 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Translating IRL to the multi-agent setting introduces new challenges, particularly regarding the concept of optimality, as each agent's strategy depends on the strategies of all other agents.
elements.sent-2411-017: This necessitates game-theoretic solution concepts, particularly equilibrium solutions.
elements.sent-2411-018: The most common of these is the Nash equilibrium \citep{goktas2024efficient, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1807-09936, ramponi2023imitation, song2022learngeneralsummarkovgames, EvaluatingStrategic, 6378020 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: In the single-agent IRL domain, there has been significant progress in understanding theoretical properties.
elements.sent-2411-020: This work was extended to settings without a generative model~\citep{lindner2023active and stricter optimality metrics~\citep{zhao2024inverse,metelli2023theoretical .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, these studies are limited to single-agent settings and compare performance based on criteria that are not directly applicable to general-sum competitive Markov games.
elements.sent-2411-022: This paper seeks to bridge the gap between the theoretical understanding of IRL in single-agent settings and its application to multi-agent systems.
elements.sent-2411-023: We extend the characterization of feasible reward sets to multi-agent settings by introducing explicit conditions for $n$-person general-sum Markov games.
elements.sent-2411-024: Additionally, we provide a comprehensive sample complexity analysis in the presence of a generative model of the transition model and the Nash experts policy.
elements.sent-2411-026: Extending the implicit conditions for feasible reward functions from two-person to $n$-person general-sum Markov games, building on \citet{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1806-09795 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Deriving an explicit characterization of the reward function feasible under a Nash-expert strategy, inspired by \citet{pmlr-v139-metelli21a .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Introducing the Nash Gap as an optimality criterion in Inverse Learning for general-sum Markov Games, extending its use beyond standard MARL.
elements.sent-2411-029: Considering the problem of recovering the feasible set of reward functions in Markov Games, we proved an upper bound on the sample complexity having access to the generative model and accounting for errors in transition probabilities and expert policy estimation.
elements.sent-2411-030: This work intersects with several fields of research, particularly Inverse Reinforcement Learning, Multi-Agent Inverse Reinforcement Learning , and Inverse (Algorithmic) Game Theory.
elements.sent-2411-031: Inverse Reinforcement Learning \quad The concept of IRL was first introduced by \cite{10.5555/645529.657801 , highlighting the ill-posed nature of the problem.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Subsequent research focused on reformulating the problem to identify a single good reward function \citep{10.1145/1015330.1015430,10.5555/1620270.1620297,10.5555/1625275.1625692,Ratliff2006BoostingSP,NIPS2011_c51ce410 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Multi-Agent Inverse Reinforcement Learning \quad The first extension of IRL to the multi-agent setting was presented by \cite{Natarajan2010MultiAgentIR .}

elements.sent-2411-044: Theoretical Understanding of IRL \quad Techniques for deriving explicit conditions for feasible reward functions and actively exploring the environment with a generative model in the single-agent IRL setting were pioneered by \cite{pmlr-v139-metelli21a .}

elements.sent-2411-046: Further, \cite{metelli2023theoretical established the first lower bounds for IRL and introduced stronger metrics for describing feasible reward sets, leading to new concentration inequalities and sample complexity bounds.}

elements.sent-2411-048: All these works and their introduced metrics consider a distance to the value function with the highest value and an expert policy maximizing this value function, making direct translation to competitive Markov games challenging as they do not consider equilibrium concepts, necessary in the presence of multiple agents.
elements.sent-2411-049: Inverse (Algorithmic) Game Theory \quad There is significant overlap between Multi-Agent Inverse Reinforcement Learning and inverse algorithmic game theory.
elements.sent-2411-050: Many works in this area apply game-theoretic solution concepts to rationalize the behavior of observed players in specific types of games (\cite{10.1007/978-3-540-92182-0_18 , \cite{5438604 ).}}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{MAIRL Uniform Sampling Algorithm with Generative Model} \label{UniS} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE Significance $\delta \in (0, 1)$, target accuracy $\epsilon$ \STATE Initialize $k \leftarrow 0$, $\epsilon_0 \leftarrow +\infty$ \WHILE{$\epsilon_k > \epsilon$} \STATE Generate one sample for each $(s, \joa) \in \calS \times \calA$ \STATE Update $\estnash_k$ for each agent $i=1, \ldots, n$ as described in \eqref{estpol} \STATE Update $\hat{P}_k$ as described in \eqref{estprop} \STATE Update accuracy $\epsilon_k \leftarrow \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \max_{(s,\joa)} \hat{C}_k(s, \joa)$ \ENDWHILE \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-170: We can now state the main result regarding the sample complexity of the Uniform Sampling algorithm for MAIRL.
elements.sent-2411-171: Some remarks are in order for this complexity bound.
elements.sent-2411-172: We observe that the sample complexity bound is exponential in the number of players.
elements.sent-2411-173: Although this might seem unfavorable, it is generally known that learning an NE in the worst case has an exponential bound.
elements.sent-2411-174: Therefore, the derived bound for the MAIRL setting aligns with the bounds derived for learning NE in the MARL setting.
elements.sent-2411-175: An extensive discussion on the bounds derived in the literature can be found in \cref{Appendix A .}
elements.sent-2411-176: Another point to notice is that for $n=2$, we can compare the result with the model-based RL zero-sum bounds derived in \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2007-07461 (compare Theorem 5).}
elements.sent-2411-177: We observe that our bound includes an additional $(1-\gamma)^{-1}$ factor.
elements.sent-2411-178: Initially, one might think this is due to the assumption of a zero-sum game in \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2007-07461 .}
elements.sent-2411-179: However, this is not the case.
elements.sent-2411-180: If we closely examine Lemma 13 in \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2007-07461 , we see that the zero-sum structure is not required for the proof.}
elements.sent-2411-181: Instead, the key point stated by the authors below the proof of Lemma 13 is that a tighter Bernstein bound is only applicable if the value function, and particularly the policy, is not dependent on the transition model of the empirical game $\hat{P}$.
elements.sent-2411-182: Since this dependency exists in our problem setting, we must apply the looser Hoeffding bound and therefore get the additional factor of $(1-\gamma)^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-183: Last, we can see that the second part of the sample complexity bound is related to estimating the expert policy.

elements.sent-2411-187: In this section, we aim to demonstrate the advantages of IRL in the multi-agent setting compared to Behavior Cloning.
elements.sent-2411-188: It is important to emphasize that the primary goal of this paper is to address the IRL problem from a theoretical perspective by defining a new objective and presenting the first algorithm to characterize the feasible set of rewards when observing a Nash expert.
elements.sent-2411-189: Since we assume the observation of a Nash expert, it is necessary to either know or compute the NE before applying the Uniform Sampling algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-190: Given that calculating the NE is PPAD-hard, our focus will be on environments where it remains feasible.
elements.sent-2411-191: The idea is to emphasize the need for MAIRL framework and motivate future research on computationally more feasible equilibrium solutions.
elements.sent-2411-192: MAIRL vs. Behavior Cloning In this paragraph, we empirically evaluate the benefits of MAIRL compared to BC.
elements.sent-2411-193: One of the advantages of IRL over BC lies in its ability to transfer the recovered reward function to new environments with different transition probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-194: This is particularly significant in a multi-agent setting, where even minor changes in transition probabilities can alter not only the individual behavior of agents but also the interactions between them.
elements.sent-2411-195: For our experiments, we utilize the $3\times3$ Gridworld example, also considered in \cite{10.5555/945365.964288 .}
elements.sent-2411-196: To recover the feasible reward set and learn the expert policy with BC, we consider a scenario where the transition probabilities are deterministic.
elements.sent-2411-197: The Nash experts are learned via NashQ-Learning as proposed in \cite{10.5555/945365.964288 .}
elements.sent-2411-198: The resulting Nash Experts and more details on the environments can be found in \ref{MAGrid .}
elements.sent-2411-199: Using the Uniform Sampling algorithm to recover the entire feasible set, we then apply a Random Max Gap MAIRL algorithm to extract a reward function from the feasible set, similar to the approach introduced in Appendix C of \cite{pmlr-v139-metelli21a .}
elements.sent-2411-200: A detailed description can be found in \cref{MaxGap .}
elements.sent-2411-201: To test the transferability of the recovered reward function, we alter the transition probabilities so that in states $(0, \text{any )$ and $(\text{any , 2)$, taking action "up" is only successful with a probability of $0.5$; otherwise, the agent remains in the same state (as in Grid Game 2 in \cite{10.5555/945365.964288 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-202: In a second scenario, we introduce an obstacle into the environment, that prohibits the agent from passing through.
elements.sent-2411-203: While in the first scenario, the BC strategy still performs reasonably, the second altered environment leads to a failure to reach the goal state for agent 1, resulting in the maximal Nash Gap.
elements.sent-2411-204: We observe that while BC may perform better in the original environment, for the first iterations, the Uniform Sampling Algorithm proves superior when transferring the reward function, especially as the number of samples increases and the environment changes.
elements.sent-2411-205: In this work, we have provided the formalization of the Inverse Reinforcement Learning problem in the multi-agent setting.
elements.sent-2411-206: Specifically, we derived implicit and explicit conditions that characterize the feasible reward set by observing Nash experts.
elements.sent-2411-207: These conditions define for which reward function the observed behaviors are in an NE.
elements.sent-2411-208: Since in practice, we do not have access to the true transition model and experts' policies, we studied the consequences of estimating these quantities.
elements.sent-2411-209: We extended bounds from the single-agent IRL setting \cite{pmlr-v139-metelli21a , showing how errors in estimating the transition model and expert policy affect the recovered reward set.}
elements.sent-2411-210: Then, to assess an agent's performance on the recovered reward, we formalized the Nash Imitation Gap \citep{ramponi2023imitation, song2022learngeneralsummarkovgames .}
elements.sent-2411-211: As part of this, we provided a new simulation lemma that accounts for the fact that Nash policies may not always maximize the value function.
elements.sent-2411-212: Building on these theoretical insights, we derived a sample complexity bound for a Uniform Sampling algorithm and empirically demonstrated the advantages of IRL over BC.
elements.sent-2411-213: This work lays the foundation for a more rigorous theoretical understanding of MAIRL.
elements.sent-2411-214: We hope that with this work researchers in the field, will use the Nash Imitation Gap as performance metrics for the MAIRL algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-215: Potential avenues for future research include deriving sample complexity bounds for alternative solution concepts and relaxing assumptions like the availability of a generative model.
elements.sent-2411-216: Exploring these directions will help further bridge the gap between theory and practical multi-agent learning applications.

elements.section-2411-009: Extensive Discussion on theoretical works of MARL
elements.sent-2411-229: In this section, we review the limited but rapidly growing body of theoretical work on MARL and compare existing sample complexity bounds with those derived in our study.
elements.sent-2411-230: In a seminal work,~\citet{doi:10.1137/070699652 established that the problem of learning Nash equilibria in 3-player zero-sum Markov games is PPAD-complete (Polynomial Parity Arguments on Directed graphs).}
elements.sent-2411-231: Subsequently,~\citet{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-0704-1678 extended this result to two-player general-sum games, showing that computing Nash equilibria remains PPAD-complete in this setting.}
elements.sent-2411-232: Among the early algorithms for $n$-player general-sum Markov games in the MARL context is the Nash Q-learning algorithm introduced by~\citet{10.5555/945365.964288 .}
elements.sent-2411-233: Their analysis demonstrated that the computational complexity of the algorithm is linear in the number of states, polynomial in the number of actions, but exponential in the number of agents.
elements.sent-2411-234: These results align with our derived sample complexity bounds, as we similarly show that it grows exponentially with the number of agents.
elements.sent-2411-235: More recent works additionally highlight that the exponential dependence on the number of agents regarding Nash equilibria is unavoidable.
elements.sent-2411-236: Consequently, many existing sample complexity analyses are restricted to more computationally tractable settings.
elements.sent-2411-237: A notable starting point for this line of work is~\citet{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2006-12007 , which introduces computationally feasible algorithms with theoretical guarantees in the MARL setting.}
elements.sent-2411-238: The authors focus on the finite-horizon, two-player, zero-sum Markov Games and propose the V-learning algorithm, deriving a sample complexity of $\Tilde{\mathcal{O (H^6 S(A+B)/\epsilon^2)$, where $A$ and $B$ represent the action space sizes for the two players, $H$ is the time horizon, and $S$ is the number of states.}}
elements.sent-2411-239: This result matches the minimax lower bound up to a $poly(H)$ factor.
elements.sent-2411-240: The key innovation is the use of both upper and lower estimates for the $V$-value, alongside techniques from bandit literature, such as Follow the Regularized Leader, integrated with standard Q-learning ideas.
elements.sent-2411-241: Building on these model-free results, which outperform existing model-based approaches,~\citet{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2010-01604 propose the Optimistic Nash Value Iteration algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-242: This algorithm achieves an $\epsilon$-NE with a sample complexity of $\Tilde{\mathcal{O (H^3 S AB / \epsilon^2)$, matching the information-theoretic lower bound $\Omega(H^3 S (A+B) / \epsilon^2)$ up to a $\min\{A,B\}$ factor.}}
elements.sent-2411-243: Furthermore, they extend their analysis to $n$-player games, proposing an algorithm that outputs policies constituting either a NE, correlated equilibrium (CE), or coarse correlated equilibrium (CCE), with a sample complexity of $\Tilde{\mathcal{O (H^4 S^2 \prod_{i \in [n]}\mathcal{A ^{i / \epsilon^2)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-244: This again underscores the inherent exponential dependence on the number of players, driven by the extensive exploration requirements in the joint action space.
elements.sent-2411-245: To address the curse of multi-agency in the general-sum setting, three concurrent works~\citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2110-05682,song2022learngeneralsummarkovgames,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2110-14555 explore decentralized algorithms, achieving sample complexity bounds that depend only on the largest action space ($\max_{i \in [n] A_i$).}}
elements.sent-2411-246: However, their algorithms are restricted to computing CE and CCE, which are known to be solvable in polynomial time–\citep{10.1145/1379759.1379762 .}
elements.sent-2411-247: In~\citet{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2110-05682 , the authors extend the V-learning algorithm and prove sample complexities of $\Tilde{\mathcal{O (H^5 S A_{\max / \epsilon^2)$ (also derived in~\citep{song2022learngeneralsummarkovgames ) for CCE and $\Tilde{\mathcal{O (H^5 S A^2_{\max}\epsilon^{-2 )$ for CE, outperforming other algorithms by a factor of $H$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-248: Similar results are achieved by a stage-based version of V-learning introduced in~\citet{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2110-05707 .}
elements.sent-2411-249: Additionally,~\citet{song2022learngeneralsummarkovgames investigate the conditions under which NE can be learned efficiently.}
elements.sent-2411-250: They consider Markov Potential Games, a subclass of Markov Games in which the change in the value function resulting from a unilateral policy change by agent $i$ can be expressed as the difference in a real-valued potential function, $\Phi$.
elements.sent-2411-251: In this setting, NE can be learned with a sample complexity that is linear in the number of players: $\Tilde{\mathcal{O (\Phi_{\max H^3 S \sum_{i \in [n] \calA^{i / \epsilon^2)$, where $\Phi_{\max \leq nH$ represents the natural bound on the potential function.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-252: This result shows that NE can be efficiently learned in this specific setting, offering a significant improvement over general-sum Markov games.
elements.sent-2411-253: As all of the aforementioned works focus on finite-horizon Markov Games, while our work addresses the infinite-horizon setting, a recent study by~\citet{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2007-07461 is of particular relevance.}
elements.sent-2411-254: They propose a model-based approach for finding NE in two-player zero-sum Markov Games, matching the single-agent model-based sample complexity bounds from~\citet{azar2017minimaxregretboundsreinforcement , with the adjusted action space size.}
elements.sent-2411-255: The authors demonstrate that, when the reward is unknown to the agent, a NE value can be computed with a sample complexity of $\Tilde{\mathcal{O (SAB (1-\gamma)^{-3 \epsilon^{-2 )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-256: Given that our bound for $n=2$ includes an additional factor of $(1-\gamma)^{-1}$, it raises the natural question of why this discrepancy exists.
elements.sent-2411-257: A potential explanation lies in the use of the tighter Bernstein concentration bounds in~\citet{azar2017minimaxregretboundsreinforcement .}
elements.sent-2411-258: However, these bounds cannot be directly applied to our setting because they require the independence of the value function and the transition probability (see Discussion below Lemma 13 in~\citet{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2007-07461 ).}
elements.sent-2411-259: A similar issue arises in the single-agent setting (see Discussion 6.2 in~\citet{pmlr-v139-metelli21a ).}
elements.sent-2411-260: Furthermore, it is important to note that in zero-sum games, the value of a NE is identical across different equilibria, whereas in general-sum Markov Games, different Nash equilibria can lead to completely different values.
elements.sent-2411-261: We have extended the Related Work section on Forward MARL works and their sample complexity bounds to better understand what constitutes a good sample complexity in the MARL setting.To the best of our knowledge, only two works address sample complexity analysis in the inverse setting and have already been discussed in detail in \cref{Related Work .}

elements.section-2411-011: Sample Complexity analysis of the Uniform Sampling algorithm
elements.sent-2411-294: In this section we give the proofs of the theorems and lemmas derived in \cref{Section 4 and \cref{Section 5 .}}
elements.sent-2411-296: We begin by stating the optimality criterion based on the Nash Imitation Gap.
elements.sent-2411-297: Next, we present the Good Event Lemma bounds, using Hoeffding's inequality.
elements.sent-2411-299: Then, we state a theorem that provides conditions—dependent on the derived confidence bounds—under which the optimality criterion holds.
elements.sent-2411-300: Finally, we consolidate all results to prove the sample complexity bound for the uniform sampling algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-301: We consider an algorithm to be \( (\epsilon, \delta, N) \)-correct after observing \( N \) samples if it holds with a probability of at least \( 1 - \delta \): \begin{equation}\sup_{R \in \feasible} \inf_{\hat{R}^i \in \estfeasible} \max_{i \in [n]} \max_{\pi^i \in \pi^i} V^{i}_{\gwr}(\pi^i, \hat{\pi}^{-i}) - V^{i}_{\gwr}(\hat{\pi}^i, \hat{\pi}^{-i}) \leq \epsilon \sup_{\hat{R} \in \feasible} \inf_{R \in \estfeasible} \max_{i \in [n]} \max_{\pi^i \in \pi^i} V^{i}_{\gwr}(\pi^i, \hat{\pi}^{-i}) - V^{i}_{\gwr}(\hat{\pi}^i, \hat{\pi}^{-i}) \leq \epsilon\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-302: Next, we state the lemma that derives the good event by applying Hoeffding's bound.
elements.sent-2411-303: Furthermore let $\estnash$ and $\hat{P}$ be the empirical estimates of the Nash expert and the transition probability after $k$ iterations as defined in \eqref{estprop and \eqref{estpol respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-304: Then for $\delta \in (0,1)$, define the good event $\mathcal{E}$ as the event such that the following inequalities hold simultaneously for all $(s,\joa) \in \calS \times \calA$ and $k\geq 1$: \begin{equation}\left\vert \mathbbm{1}_{E} - \mathbbm{1}_{\hat{E}} A^i(s,\joa) \right\vert \leq \frac{\maxR}{1-\gamma} \mathbbm{1}_{\{N_k(s) \geq \max\{1, \xi(N_k(s), \delta/2)\}\}}, \left\vert \mathbbm{1}_{E} - \mathbbm{1}_{\hat{E}} \hat{A}^i(s,\joa) \right\vert \leq \frac{\maxR}{1-\gamma} \mathbbm{1}_{\{N_k(s) \geq \max\{1, \xi(N_k(s), \delta/2)\}\}}, \sum_{s'} \left\vert (P(s' \mid s,\joa) - \hat{P}_k(s' \mid s,\joa)) V^i(s') \right\vert \leq \frac{\maxR}{1-\gamma} \sqrt{\frac{2 l_k(s,\joa)}{N_{k}^{+}(s,\joa)}}, \sum_{s'} \left\vert (P(s' \mid s,\joa) - \hat{P}_k(s' \mid s,\joa)) \hat{V}^i(s') \right\vert \leq \frac{\maxR}{1-\gamma} \sqrt{\frac{2 l_k(s,\joa)}{N_{k}^{+}(s,\joa)}}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-306: Therefore we define $l_k(s,\joa) = \log\left(\frac{12S\prod_i \vert\calA^{i}\vert (N_{k ^{+ (s,\joa))^2 {\delta}\right)$ and additionally we denote $\beta_{N_k(s,\joa) = \frac{ \maxR {1-\gamma \sqrt{\frac{2 l_k(s,\joa) {N_{k ^{+ (s,\joa) }$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-307: Now we define the set \begin{equation}E^{trans} := \left\{ \forall k \in N, \, \forall (s,\joa) \in \calS \times \calA: \sum_{s' \in \calS} \vert (P(s' \mid s,\joa) - \hat{P}(s' \mid s, \joa)) V^i(s')\vert \leq \beta_{N_k(s,\joa)} \right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-308: Then we get for $V^{i}$ with probability of $1-\delta$: \begin{equation}P\left((E^{trans})^C\right) = P\left(\exists k \geq 1, \exists(s,\joa)\in \calS \times \calA: \sum_{s'}\left\vert \left( P(s' \mid s, \joa)-\hat{P}_k(s' \mid s, \joa) \right)V^{i}(s') \right\vert > \beta_{N_k(s,\joa)}(s,\joa)\right) \quad \overset{(a)}{\leq} \sum_{(s,\joa) \in \calS \times \calA}P\left(\exists k \geq 1: \sum_{s'}\left\vert \left( P(s' \mid s, \joa)-\hat{P}_k(s' \mid s, \joa) \right)V^{i}(s') \right\vert > \beta_{N_k(s,\joa)}(s,\joa)\right) \quad \overset{(b)}{\leq} \sum_{(s,\joa) \in \calS \times \calA}P\left(\exists m \geq 0: \sum_{s'}\left\vert \left( P(s' \mid s, \joa)-\hat{P}_k(s' \mid s, \joa) \right)V^{i}(s') \right\vert > \beta_{N_k(s,\joa)}(s,\joa)\right) \quad \overset{(c)}{\leq} \sum_{(s,\joa) \in \calS \times \calA} \sum_{m \geq 0} 2 \exp\left(\frac{ \beta_{N_k(s, \joa)}^2 m^2 (1-\gamma)^2 }{4 m \gamma^2 R_{\max}^2}\right) \quad \leq \sum_{(s,\joa) \in \calS \times \calA} \sum_{m \geq 0} \frac{\delta}{6 S (\prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert\calA^{i}\vert) (m^+)^2} \quad \leq \frac{\delta}{6}\left(1 + \frac{\pi^2}{6}\right) \leq \frac{\delta}{2},\end{equation} where (a) uses a union bound over the state and joint-action space, (b) uses that we only consider the $m$-times, where we updated the estimated transition model and (c) uses an union bound over the update times $m$ and an application of Hoeffding's inequality combined with the fact that we can bound the value function, i.e. $V^{i (s') \leq \frac{\maxR {1-\gamma}$ for every $s' \in \calS$.}}
elements.formula-2411-033: \begin{equation}P\left((E^{trans})^C\right) = P\left(\exists k \geq 1, \exists(s,\joa)\in \calS \times \calA: \sum_{s'}\left\vert \left( P(s' \mid s, \joa)-\hat{P}_k(s' \mid s, \joa) \right)V^{i}(s') \right\vert > \beta_{N_k(s,\joa)}(s,\joa)\right) \quad \overset{(a)}{\leq} \sum_{(s,\joa) \in \calS \times \calA}P\left(\exists k \geq 1: \sum_{s'}\left\vert \left( P(s' \mid s, \joa)-\hat{P}_k(s' \mid s, \joa) \right)V^{i}(s') \right\vert > \beta_{N_k(s,\joa)}(s,\joa)\right) \quad \overset{(b)}{\leq} \sum_{(s,\joa) \in \calS \times \calA}P\left(\exists m \geq 0: \sum_{s'}\left\vert \left( P(s' \mid s, \joa)-\hat{P}_k(s' \mid s, \joa) \right)V^{i}(s') \right\vert > \beta_{N_k(s,\joa)}(s,\joa)\right) \quad \overset{(c)}{\leq} \sum_{(s,\joa) \in \calS \times \calA} \sum_{m \geq 0} 2 \exp\left(\frac{ \beta_{N_k(s, \joa)}^2 m^2 (1-\gamma)^2 }{4 m \gamma^2 R_{\max}^2}\right) \quad \leq \sum_{(s,\joa) \in \calS \times \calA} \sum_{m \geq 0} \frac{\delta}{6 S (\prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert\calA^{i}\vert) (m^+)^2} \quad \leq \frac{\delta}{6}\left(1 + \frac{\pi^2}{6}\right) \leq \frac{\delta}{2},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-309: Next, we will consider the first two equations regarding the estimation of the Nash policy of the expert.

elements.sent-2411-450: In multi-agent systems, agent behavior is driven by utility functions that encapsulate their individual goals and interactions.
elements.sent-2411-451: Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) seeks to uncover these utilities by analyzing expert behavior, offering insights into the underlying decision-making processes.
elements.sent-2411-452: However, multi-agent settings pose significant challenges, particularly when rewards are inferred from equilibrium observations.
elements.sent-2411-453: A key obstacle is that single (Nash) equilibrium observations often fail to adequately capture critical game properties, leading to potential misrepresentations.
elements.sent-2411-454: This paper offers a rigorous analysis of the feasible reward set in multi-agent IRL and addresses these limitations by introducing entropy-regularized games, ensuring equilibrium uniqueness and enhancing interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-455: Furthermore, we examine the effects of estimation errors and present the first sample complexity results for multi-agent IRL across diverse scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-456: To address this, subsequent research has focused on reformulating the IRL problem to make it more practical and applicable in real-world settings~\citep{10.1145/1015330.1015430,10.5555/1620270.1620297,10.5555/1625275.1625692,Ratliff2006BoostingSP .}
elements.sent-2411-457: Translating IRL to the multi-agent setting introduces new challenges, particularly regarding the concept of optimality and the diversity of Nash equilibria, as each agent's strategy depends on the strategies of all other agents.
elements.sent-2411-458: The most common of these is the Nash equilibrium \citep{goktas2024efficient, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1807-09936, ramponi2023imitation, EvaluatingStrategic .}
elements.sent-2411-459: The theoretical foundations of multi-agent Inverse Reinforcement Learning remain relatively underexplored, in contrast to the significant progress made in understanding theoretical properties within the single-agent IRL domain.
elements.sent-2411-460: This work was extended to settings without a generative model~\citep{lindner2023active and stricter optimality metrics~\citep{zhao2024inverse,metelli2023theoretical and the offline setting~\cite{lazzati2024offlineinverserlnew, zhao2024inverse .}}}
elements.sent-2411-461: Deriving an explicit characterization of the reward function feasible under a (pure) Nash-expert strategy in general-sum Markov Games, inspired by \citet{pmlr-v139-metelli21a .}
elements.sent-2411-462: Demonstrating that observing a single equilibrium solution does not provide sufficient information to construct an expressive set of feasible rewards.

elements.section-2411-014: Feasible Reward Set in Multi-Agent systems
elements.sent-2411-492: In single-agent IRL the first definition of a feasible reward set was given in \citet{pmlr-v139-metelli21a .}
elements.sent-2411-493: After this, slight derivations of the feasible reward sets have been introduced~\citep{zhao2024inverse, metelli2023theoretical, lazzati2024offlineinverserlnew .}
elements.sent-2411-494: To the best of our knowledge, there exists no characterization of the feasible reward set in multi-agent systems, when observing an equilibrium solution as an expert.
elements.sent-2411-495: Next, we give a straightforward translation of the definition of the feasible reward set from the single agent setting.
elements.sent-2411-496: This definition expresses formally the feasible rewards considered in previous works \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/LinBC14,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1806-09795 .}
elements.sent-2411-497: Let $\Nash$ be the observed Nash equilibrium expert and a stochastic game $\mathcal{G}$ be given.
elements.sent-2411-498: Then the feasible reward set for general-sum Markov Games is given by \begin{equation}\gR^{feas} = \left\{R \in \gR \mid \forall i \in [n], \forall s \in \gS, \forall \pi_i \in \Pi_i: \right. \left.V^{\pi_i^{Nash},\pi_{-i}^{Nash} }_{\gG \cup R}(s) > V^{\pi_i,\pi_{-i}^{Nash} }_{\gG \cup R}(s)\right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-499: Note that $\Nash = (\pi_i^{\text{Nash , \pi_{-i ^{\text{Nash ) \in \Pi^{\text{Nash }$ is any Nash equilibrium strategy from the set of all Nash equilibrium strategies $\Pi^{\text{Nash }$, similar to the single agent case, where the expert is any policy maximizing the Value function.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-500: It is important to note that in Markov Games, different Nash equilibria have different values and we do not make any restriction on which equilibrium is chosen from the set of all Nash equilibrium strategies.
elements.sent-2411-501: This is the first fundamental difference between IRL and Multi-agent IRL (MAIRL).
elements.sent-2411-502: The observed Nash expert, could therefore be a pure Nash equilibrium or a fully mixed one.
elements.sent-2411-503: As a warm-up we derive an explicit characterization of the reward function for each player, when observing a pure NE strategy.
elements.sent-2411-504: Next, we need to define an objective for MAIRL.
elements.sent-2411-505: As already mentioned, different Nash equilibria can have different values, therefore the objectives of single-agent IRL, which are all value-based gaps~\citep{pmlr-v139-metelli21a,metelli2023theoretical,zhao2024inverse,lazzati2024offlineinverserlnew , can not directly be translated to multi-agent settings.}

elements.section-2411-015: Feasible Reward Set and Equilibrium Ambiguity
elements.sent-2411-516: In this section, we show that observing only a single equilibrium solution leads to a non-expressive feasible reward set.
elements.sent-2411-517: Here, we extend this finding to the feasible reward set, rather than focusing solely on specific reward functions.
elements.sent-2411-518: Next, we will introduce a running example, that helps to understand the key properties of MAIRL.
elements.sent-2411-519: We consider the general form of a coordination game as a Normal Form Game(NFG): \begin{center \begin{equation}{c|c|c} Player 2: Stag Player 2: Hare Player 1: Stag (A, A) (C, B) Player 1: Hare (B, C) (D, D) \end{equation} \end{center In general coordination games, we have that $D > B$ and $D-B < A+D-B-C$.}}
elements.formula-2411-079: \begin{equation}{c|c|c} Player 2: Stag Player 2: Hare Player 1: Stag (A, A) (C, B) Player 1: Hare (B, C) (D, D) \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-520: Assume we observe the pure Nash equilibrium strategy \((\text{Stag , \text{Stag )\).}}
elements.sent-2411-521: The feasible reward set \(\gR^{\text{feas }\) then contains all rewards that satisfy: \begin{equation}R^1(Stag, Stag) \geq R^1(Hare, Stag) \land R^2(Stag, Stag) \geq R^2(Stag, Hare),\end{equation} while for all other reward values, any rewards are feasible, i.e., \(R^1(\text{Stag , \text{Hare )\), \(R^1(\text{Hare , \text{Hare ) \in \mathbb{R ^{\gA_1 \times \gA_2}\).}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-523: Changing the nature of the game: The game can transform into an anti-coordination variant with additional pure Nash equilibria not present in the original game.
elements.sent-2411-524: Losing equilibria: Rewards can be defined so that "Stag" becomes the unique dominant strategy for player 1.

elements.sent-2411-526: This example highlights that even in simple NFGs, the feasible reward set encompasses too many reward functions, including those that significantly alter the game's equilibria.
elements.sent-2411-527: This contrasts with the single-agent IRL setting, where the feasible reward set contains degenerate rewards like constant ones, but due to the fact that all equilibria obtain the same value, preserving the meaning of the environment.
elements.sent-2411-528: In the multi-agent setting, this second source of ambiguity allows for strategic behavior entirely absent in the original game, which is highly undesirable if the goal is to recover meaningful reward functions for transfer to new environments.
elements.sent-2411-529: To address this, we propose the following definition of the feasible reward set:.
elements.sent-2411-530: Let \(\Nash\) denote the observed Nash equilibrium expert and \(\mathcal{G}\) be a stochastic game.
elements.sent-2411-531: Then, the feasible reward set for general-sum Markov Games is defined as: \begin{equation}\gR^{feas} = \left\{R \in \gR \mid \forall \Nash \in \Pi^{Nash}, \forall i \in [n], \forall s \in \gS, \forall \pi_i \in \Pi_i:\VNash_{\gG \cup R}(s) > V^{\pi_i, \pi_{-i}^{Nash} }_{\gG \cup R}(s)\right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-532: This definition ensures that the feasible reward set aligns with all equilibrium solutions of the original game, rather than a single observed equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-533: While this improves the interpretability of the feasible reward set, calculating even one Nash equilibrium is computationally intractable in general-sum Markov Games.
elements.sent-2411-534: Hence, this definition does not fully resolve the tractability issue in MAIRL.
elements.sent-2411-535: The multiplicity of equilibria and the absence of a unique value pose a significant challenge in Inverse Reinforcement Learning, commonly referred to as the Equilibrium Selection problem.
elements.sent-2411-536: This ambiguity complicates reward inference, as different equilibria can lead to inconsistent or unreliable outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-537: To address this,~\citet{leonardos2021explorationexploitationmultiagentcompetitionconvergence propose modifying the game structure to ensure the existence of a unique Nash equilibrium.}
elements.sent-2411-538: Specifically, they leverage regularized Markov Games, where entropy regularization or similar techniques refine the equilibrium set, guaranteeing uniqueness.
elements.sent-2411-539: This effectively eliminates the need for equilibrium selection, as the regularization ensures that only a single equilibrium remains viable.
elements.sent-2411-540: By resolving the ambiguity in equilibrium selection, regularized Markov Games allow the focus to shift towards accurately recovering the underlying reward structure governing agent behavior.
elements.sent-2411-541: This approach simplifies the multi-agent IRL framework, addressing the core challenges posed by equilibrium multiplicity and providing a clearer foundation for learning meaningful rewards in competitive multi-agent settings.
elements.sent-2411-542: In this section, we focus on Entropy-Regularized Markov Games, a framework that facilitates the establishment of a unique equilibrium solution known as the Quantal Response Equilibrium (QRE).
elements.sent-2411-543: This equilibrium represents a probabilistic counterpart to the Nash equilibrium, modeling players as having bounded rationality---that is, they make decisions based on beliefs about the strategies of other agents rather than complete certainty.
elements.sent-2411-544: First, we show that in our running example, the undesirable reward cases like the ones given in Stag-Hunt are not possible.
elements.sent-2411-545: Then, we argue that to characterize the feasible reward set, our first step is to derive an explicit representation of the reward function.
elements.sent-2411-546: However, we begin by proving a key limitation: an explicit reward function, that does not depend on other rewards is derivable only in the average case of entropy-regularized General-Sum Markov games, even when assuming symmetry or a Zero-Sum structure.
elements.sent-2411-547: This result highlights the inherent complexity introduced by multi-agent systems.
elements.sent-2411-548: In the latter part of this section, we propose conditions under which an explicit reward function can be derived.
elements.sent-2411-549: Specifically, we introduce and analyze the concept of linear separability of rewards, which enables the development of a tractable formulation.
elements.sent-2411-550: Through this analysis, we aim to bridge the gap between theoretical feasibility and practical computation in reward modeling for entropy-regularized games.
elements.sent-2411-551: In this part, we first want to show, that adding the entropy regularization and therefore giving us a unique fully mixed equilibrium, gives us enough structure about recovering the reward, such that the degenerate cases, given in \cref{Fig:antiexample , can be avoided.}
elements.sent-2411-552: To do so we assume to know the regularization parameter $\lambda_1=0.5, \lambda_2=1$\footnote{To be precise, this is a refinement of the QRE as the QRE corresponds to the case $\lambda_1=\lambda_2$} and additionally we know the optimal strategies of both players $\mu^*(\text{Stag )=0.78$ and $\nu^*(\text{Stag )=0.89$, coming from a symmetric reward structure as assumed in the general Stag Hunt game \cref{Fig:Stag Hunt , in particular for the specific reward values $A=3, D=1, B=C=0$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-553: A simple computation then results in receiving the following structure for the possible rewards \begin{equation}0.89(B-A) + 0.11(D-C) = -2.58;\end{equation} \begin{equation}0.78(B-A) + 0.22(D-C) = -2.14;\end{equation} Even though that the equation is not solvable for specific values for $A,B,C,D$, we can subtract the equations from each other to say something about the relations of the rewards, namely $(B-A) = 3$ where plugging it into the original equations, then results in $D-C \approx 1.$ Most importantly, reward values like in \cref{Fig:antiexample are not possible.}
elements.sent-2411-554: It is also important to point out, that simply taking the mixed Nash equilibrium strategy of the unregularized Game, would give us similar information compared to the case $\lambda_1=\lambda_2$, but because of the equilibrium selection problem, we can not force such a condition in general.
elements.sent-2411-555: Nonetheless, we see that identification of the reward function is not possible in simple Normal Form Games even if we know the optimal strategies and the parametrization values.

elements.sent-2411-620: In this work, we formalized the Inverse Reinforcement Learning problem in the multi-agent setting, highlighting the unique challenges compared to single-agent IRL.
elements.sent-2411-621: Specifically, we demonstrated that single equilibrium observations are insufficient for constructing a meaningful reward function due to the multiplicity of equilibria and the ambiguity in selecting an appropriate one.
elements.sent-2411-622: To address this, we restricted the feasible reward set to regularized Markov Games, ensuring equilibrium uniqueness.
elements.sent-2411-623: While identifiability remains unattainable without additional assumptions, we investigated practical scenarios where the true transition model and expert policies are not directly accessible.
elements.sent-2411-624: We extended single-agent IRL bounds~\cite{pmlr-v139-metelli21a , analyzing how errors in estimating these quantities propagate to the recovered reward set.}
elements.sent-2411-625: Furthermore, we introduced the Nash Imitation Gap~\citep{ramponi2023imitation, song2022learngeneralsummarkovgames, tang2024multiagentimitationlearningvalue as a performance metric within MAIRL, formalizing a practical measure to evaluate agents under recovered rewards.}
elements.sent-2411-626: Building on these insights, we derived a sample complexity bound for the Uniform Sampling algorithm for both pure Nash and Quantal Response equilibria.
elements.sent-2411-627: Thus, while MAIRL is inherently more complex than its single-agent counterpart, it also offers richer information under certain assumptions, facilitating more structured reward inference.
elements.sent-2411-628: Additionally, we conducted experiments on simple games with pure Nash equilibria (\cref{Appendix:Experiments ).}
elements.sent-2411-629: This work establishes a theoretical foundation for advancing MAIRL, addressing both fundamental and practical aspects.
elements.sent-2411-630: We hope our introduction of the Nash Imitation Gap will encourage researchers to adopt it as a standard performance metric.
elements.sent-2411-631: Exploring these directions will deepen the understanding of MAIRL and bridge the gap between theoretical advancements and practical applications in multi-agent learning.. \bibliographystyle{plainnat \onecolumn.}
